title: Software Developers
task: Analyze information to determine, recommend, and plan installation of a new system or modification of an existing system.
rationale: This task is dominated by Interpretation—synthesizing requirements, constraints, and technical options to form a coherent system recommendation—supported by Knowledge of systems architecture and Process control over the analysis-to-recommendation workflow, with modest Technical skill in evaluating implementation feasibility.

title: Software Developers
task: Analyze user needs and software requirements to determine feasibility of design within time and cost constraints.
rationale: This task is dominated by Interpretation—synthesizing ambiguous user needs, software requirements, and constraints into a feasibility judgment—supported by domain Knowledge about software design and technology trade-offs, with minor contributions from Process (structuring the analysis workflow), Credential (signaling competence), Technical (understanding implementation realities), and Relational (accountability for the assessment).

title: Software Developers
task: Confer with data processing or project managers to obtain information on limitations or capabilities for data processing projects.
rationale: This conferral task centers on synthesizing and interpreting technical constraints from stakeholders (I), leveraging domain knowledge to ask the right questions (K), and accessing information gatekept by project managers (A), with modest relational and process components for navigating organizational workflow.

title: Software Developers
task: Confer with systems analysts, engineers, programmers and others to design systems and to obtain information on project limitations and capabilities, performance requirements and interfaces.
rationale: This conferencing/design task is dominated by Interpretation (synthesizing constraints, requirements, and interfaces from multiple stakeholders into coherent system design decisions) and Process (structuring and gatekeeping the design workflow), with Knowledge supporting the technical vocabulary needed to evaluate tradeoffs.

title: Software Developers
task: Consult with customers or other departments on project status, proposals, or technical issues, such as software system design or maintenance.
rationale: Consulting on project status and technical issues is dominated by Interpretation (synthesizing technical complexity into actionable guidance), Knowledge (domain expertise the client lacks), and Relational (sustained stakeholder trust and accountability), with modest Process and Technical contributions for framing workflows and communicating implementation realities.

title: Software Developers
task: Coordinate installation of software system.
rationale: Coordinating a software installation is predominantly a process-control task—sequencing steps, managing dependencies, and orchestrating stakeholders—with secondary technical execution (running installers, configuring environments) and interpretation (resolving conflicts, adapting to environment specifics).

title: Software Developers
task: Design, develop and modify software systems, using scientific analysis and mathematical models to predict and measure outcomes and consequences of design.
rationale: This task centers on hands-on coding and system construction (T), with significant interpretation effort in translating scientific/mathematical models and requirements into design specifications (I), supported by domain knowledge of algorithms and systems (K), and minor process control over the design-develop-modify lifecycle (P).

title: Software Developers
task: Determine system performance standards.
rationale: Determining system performance standards is primarily an interpretive task—synthesizing business requirements, system constraints, and technical tradeoffs into concrete benchmarks—grounded in domain knowledge about performance metrics, latency, throughput, and scalability patterns.

title: Software Developers
task: Develop or direct software system testing or validation procedures, programming, or documentation.
rationale: This task centers on controlling the testing/validation workflow (P) and hands-on technical execution of test procedures and documentation (T), with meaningful interpretation in translating requirements into test plans (I), and minor contributions from domain knowledge, credentials, and accountability.

title: Software Developers
task: Modify existing software to correct errors, adapt it to new hardware, or upgrade interfaces and improve performance.
rationale: Modifying existing software is dominated by hands-on debugging and coding (Technical), with significant interpretive judgment in diagnosing errors and translating requirements into fixes (Interpretation), supported by domain knowledge of the codebase and languages (Knowledge), and minor process/relational overhead.

title: Software Developers
task: Monitor functioning of equipment to ensure system operates in conformance with specifications.
rationale: Monitoring equipment conformance is primarily a process-control activity (watching pipelines, logs, alerts) requiring technical skill to interpret system outputs and diagnose anomalies, plus domain knowledge of what constitutes correct behavior against specs.

title: Software Developers
task: Obtain and evaluate information on factors such as reporting formats required, costs, or security needs to determine hardware configuration.
rationale: This task is dominated by Interpretation (synthesizing reporting formats, costs, and security needs into a hardware configuration decision) backed by Knowledge of hardware/software constraints, with modest Process involvement in structuring the evaluation workflow.

title: Software Developers
task: Prepare reports or correspondence concerning project specifications, activities, or status.
rationale: Preparing project reports and correspondence is primarily about synthesizing and interpreting project status into coherent communication (I), controlling the documentation workflow and structure (P), with moderate technical skill in articulating specifications clearly (T) and some relational accountability to stakeholders (R).

title: Software Developers
task: Recommend purchase of equipment to control dust, temperature, or humidity in area of system installation.
rationale: This task is primarily about applying domain knowledge of environmental conditions affecting hardware (dust, temperature, humidity) and interpreting site-specific requirements to synthesize an appropriate equipment recommendation.

title: Software Developers
task: Specify power supply requirements and configuration.
rationale: Specifying power supply requirements and configuration is dominated by domain knowledge (hardware specs, voltage/current tolerances, standards) and interpretive judgment (translating system requirements into precise power specifications), with minor contributions from other channels.

title: Software Developers
task: Store, retrieve, and manipulate data for analysis of system capabilities and requirements.
rationale: This task centers on the technical execution of data storage/retrieval/manipulation (writing queries, scripts, or code) combined with interpretation of system capabilities and requirements to determine what data to capture and how to analyze it, with secondary process and knowledge components.

title: Software Developers
task: Supervise and assign work to programmers, designers, technologists, technicians, or other engineering or scientific personnel.
rationale: Supervising and assigning work to technical staff is primarily about controlling and coordinating the workflow (task allocation, sequencing, oversight), with meaningful relational accountability for team outcomes and some interpretive judgment in matching tasks to personnel capabilities.

title: Software Developers
task: Supervise the work of programmers, technologists and technicians and other engineering and scientific personnel.
rationale: Supervising programmers and technical personnel is primarily about controlling workflow and approval processes (P), maintaining accountability and trusted relationships with team members and stakeholders (R), with meaningful credential signaling (C) and interpretive judgment in assigning and evaluating work (I).

title: Software Developers
task: Train users to use new or modified equipment.
rationale: Training users on new/modified equipment primarily draws on Knowledge (explaining how the system works), Process (structuring the training workflow and curriculum delivery), and Interpretation (adapting explanations to user needs), with minor contributions from other channels.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Identify, analyze, and document problems with program function, output, online screen, or content.
rationale: This task centers on interpreting expected vs. actual program behavior and synthesizing findings into actionable documentation, with significant technical skill in executing tests and reproducing bugs, backed by domain knowledge of software behavior and quality standards.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Document software defects, using a bug tracking system, and report defects to software developers.
rationale: This task centers on controlling the defect-tracking workflow (P) and synthesizing observations into clear, reproducible bug reports (I), with moderate technical skill in using tracking systems and reproducing defects (T), and minor domain knowledge of what constitutes a defect (K).

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Develop testing programs that address areas such as database impacts, software scenarios, regression testing, negative testing, error or bug retests, or usability.
rationale: Developing testing programs requires translating requirements into test specifications (Interpretation), hands-on construction of test scripts and frameworks (Technical), knowledge of testing methodologies like regression and negative testing (Knowledge), and structuring the overall test workflow (Process).

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Design test plans, scenarios, scripts, or procedures.
rationale: Designing test plans and scripts requires heavy interpretation (translating requirements into test coverage logic) and process control (structuring workflows, coverage strategies, gating criteria), with moderate technical skill in scripting and domain knowledge of testing methodologies.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Document test procedures to ensure replicability and compliance with standards.
rationale: Documenting test procedures is primarily about controlling and formalizing a workflow (Process), with meaningful contributions from synthesizing requirements into replicable steps (Interpretation), applied QA knowledge (Knowledge), and the hands-on writing of structured procedural documentation (Technical).

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Provide feedback and recommendations to developers on software usability and functionality.
rationale: Providing feedback and recommendations on usability and functionality is dominated by Interpretation—synthesizing test findings, user experience observations, and product requirements into actionable, prioritized guidance—supported by Knowledge of QA standards and software behavior, and modest Technical skill in identifying root causes.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Install, maintain, or use software testing programs.
rationale: Installing, maintaining, and using software testing programs is primarily a hands-on technical execution task (setting up tools, configuring environments, running tests) with meaningful process control over the testing workflow, and modest knowledge of testing frameworks.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Test system modifications to prepare for implementation.
rationale: Testing system modifications is primarily a hands-on technical execution task (writing/running test cases, debugging) with significant process control (managing the test workflow and release gates) and interpretation (translating modification specs into test scenarios), while other channels play minor supporting roles.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Create or maintain databases of known test defects.
rationale: Creating and maintaining defect databases is primarily a workflow/process control task (organizing, categorizing, and managing defect records) with significant technical execution (database setup, querying, data entry tooling) and moderate interpretation (classifying and reconciling defect information).

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Develop or specify standards, methods, or procedures to determine product quality or release readiness.
rationale: Developing QA standards and release-readiness procedures is primarily an interpretive act (translating ambiguous product requirements into concrete criteria) and a process-control act (defining the workflow and approval gates), grounded in domain knowledge of testing methodologies.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Monitor bug resolution efforts and track successes.
rationale: Monitoring and tracking bug resolution is primarily a workflow-control activity (managing queues, statuses, gates) with significant interpretive judgment about prioritization and progress, plus some technical knowledge of bug systems and root causes.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Update automated test scripts to ensure currency.
rationale: Updating automated test scripts is primarily a hands-on technical coding task (modifying scripts to reflect current system behavior), with meaningful interpretation involved in understanding what changes in the application require script updates, and some process awareness of how tests fit into CI/CD pipelines.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Participate in product design reviews to provide input on functional requirements, product designs, schedules, or potential problems.
rationale: Participating in design reviews is primarily about synthesizing functional requirements, design specs, and schedules to identify potential problems (Interpretation), supported by domain knowledge of QA principles, with minor process influence as a gating voice in the review workflow.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Plan test schedules or strategies in accordance with project scope or delivery dates.
rationale: Planning test schedules and strategies is primarily about controlling the QA workflow (P) and synthesizing project scope/constraints into a coherent test plan (I), with domain knowledge of testing approaches supporting both.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Monitor program performance to ensure efficient and problem-free operations.
rationale: Monitoring program performance centers on controlling the observability/alerting workflow (P), interpreting metrics and logs to distinguish normal from problematic behavior (I), with supporting technical skill in tooling and domain knowledge of system behavior patterns.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Conduct software compatibility tests with programs, hardware, operating systems, or network environments.
rationale: Compatibility testing is dominated by hands-on technical execution (setting up environments, running tests, debugging failures), process control (managing test suites and environments), and knowledge of platforms/dependencies, with moderate interpretation in diagnosing cross-environment issues.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Investigate customer problems referred by technical support.
rationale: Investigating customer-reported problems requires heavy interpretation (synthesizing vague customer complaints, logs, and system behavior into a diagnosis), meaningful technical skill (reproducing bugs, tracing code paths, debugging), and process control (triaging and managing investigation workflow), with background knowledge of the software domain.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Review software documentation to ensure technical accuracy, compliance, or completeness, or to mitigate risks.
rationale: Reviewing documentation for accuracy, compliance, and completeness is primarily an interpretive task—synthesizing technical content against standards and requirements—supported by domain knowledge and light process control over the review workflow.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Identify program deviance from standards, and suggest modifications to ensure compliance.
rationale: This task is dominated by Interpretation (judging where code/behavior deviates from standards and synthesizing remediation suggestions) and Knowledge (knowing the relevant standards and quality criteria), with Process reflecting the structured QA workflow and minor Technical execution in examining code/behavior.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Perform initial debugging procedures by reviewing configuration files, logs, or code pieces to determine breakdown source.
rationale: Initial debugging is dominated by hands-on technical skill (navigating logs, configs, code) and interpretive synthesis (correlating multiple sources to locate a breakdown), with supporting domain knowledge of system architecture and error patterns.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Design or develop automated testing tools.
rationale: Designing and developing automated testing tools is dominated by hands-on coding and technical execution (T), with meaningful interpretation required to translate testing requirements into tool architecture and logic (I), and supporting domain knowledge of testing frameworks and software systems (K).

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Install and configure recreations of software production environments to allow testing of software performance.
rationale: This task is dominated by hands-on technical execution (installing, configuring, and deploying environment infrastructure) with significant process control (managing the recreation pipeline/workflow) and supporting domain knowledge of production stack configurations.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Collaborate with field staff or customers to evaluate or diagnose problems and recommend possible solutions.
rationale: This task centers on diagnosing ambiguous problems and synthesizing solutions (Interpretation), drawing on QA domain knowledge (Knowledge), with moderate relational trust and technical debugging skill required to credibly engage with field staff and customers.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Coordinate user or third-party testing.
rationale: Coordinating user or third-party testing is primarily about controlling the workflow—scheduling, organizing, and managing the testing process—with secondary emphasis on interpreting requirements and bridging stakeholders, and minor contributions from domain knowledge, access to test participants, and relational accountability.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Visit beta testing sites to evaluate software performance.
rationale: Beta site visits primarily require interpreting real-world software performance against expected behavior, controlling the evaluation process/workflow, accessing live deployment environments, and applying domain knowledge to judge defects and edge cases.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Conduct historical analyses of test results.
rationale: Historical analysis of test results centers on synthesizing patterns and drawing meaningful conclusions (Interpretation) from accumulated data, governed by structured analytical workflows (Process), with supporting domain knowledge about QA metrics and some technical skill in querying/manipulating test data.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Evaluate or recommend software for testing or bug tracking.
rationale: Evaluating and recommending testing/bug-tracking software is primarily a judgment task (Interpretation) requiring synthesis of tool capabilities, team needs, and project context, grounded in substantive domain knowledge (Knowledge) of the QA tooling landscape.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Modify existing software to correct errors, allow it to adapt to new hardware, or to improve its performance.
rationale: Modifying existing software to fix errors or improve performance is dominated by hands-on coding and debugging (Technical), with meaningful Interpretation needed to understand error causes and translate hardware/performance requirements into code changes, plus background Knowledge of software architecture and systems.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Recommend purchase of equipment to control dust, temperature, or humidity in area of system installation.
rationale: This task is dominated by domain knowledge about environmental requirements for hardware/systems (temperature, humidity, dust tolerances) and interpretive judgment in diagnosing environmental conditions and matching them to appropriate equipment solutions.

title: Software Quality Assurance Analysts and Testers
task: Store, retrieve, and manipulate data for analysis of system capabilities and requirements.
rationale: This task centers on the hands-on technical execution of data storage, retrieval, and manipulation (queries, scripts, data pipelines) combined with process control over how data flows into analysis, with interpretation needed to translate system capability requirements into appropriate data structures and queries.

title: Web Developers
task: Write supporting code for Web applications or Web sites.
rationale: Writing supporting code (backend logic, APIs, utilities) for web applications is dominated by hands-on coding and debugging (Technical), with meaningful Interpretation in translating requirements into working implementations, and moderate Knowledge of languages, frameworks, and patterns.

title: Web Developers
task: Design, build, or maintain Web sites, using authoring or scripting languages, content creation tools, management tools, and digital media.
rationale: Building and maintaining websites centers on hands-on coding and deployment (T), with meaningful interpretation in translating requirements into architecture and implementation choices (I), supported by domain knowledge of languages and tools (K), and modest process control over the development workflow (P).

title: Web Developers
task: Back up files from Web sites to local directories for instant recovery in case of problems.
rationale: Backing up website files is primarily a procedural workflow task (setting up backup scripts, schedules, directory structures) combined with hands-on technical execution, with minimal interpretation or relational components.

title: Web Developers
task: Select programming languages, design tools, or applications.
rationale: Selecting programming languages and tools is primarily a knowledge-intensive judgment call (knowing the landscape of options, tradeoffs, and fit) combined with interpretive synthesis of project requirements against available tools, with minor process and credential weight.

title: Web Developers
task: Evaluate code to ensure that it is valid, is properly structured, meets industry standards, and is compatible with browsers, devices, or operating systems.
rationale: Code evaluation is primarily a technical task (running linters, validators, testing cross-browser/device compatibility) grounded in substantive knowledge of standards (HTML/CSS/JS specs, browser quirks), with meaningful interpretation in reconciling industry standards against actual code structure, and some process control over the review workflow.

title: Web Developers
task: Develop databases that support Web applications and Web sites.
rationale: Developing databases for web applications is heavily weighted toward Technical execution (schema design, query optimization, hands-on implementation) and Knowledge of database systems, with some Interpretation needed to translate application requirements into database structures and modest Process for managing the development workflow.

title: Web Developers
task: Perform Web site tests according to planned schedules, or after any Web site or product revision.
rationale: Website testing is primarily a process-controlled workflow (scheduling, executing test plans, managing revision cycles) combined with technical execution (running tests, debugging failures, interpreting results), with modest interpretation needed to assess what constitutes a pass/fail and minor knowledge of testing standards.

title: Web Developers
task: Perform or direct Web site updates.
rationale: Website updates are dominated by technical execution (coding, deploying changes) and process control (managing the update workflow/release pipeline), with moderate interpretation to translate requirements into implementation and some relational accountability for ongoing site health.

title: Web Developers
task: Maintain understanding of current Web technologies or programming practices through continuing education, reading, or participation in professional conferences, workshops, or groups.
rationale: This task is fundamentally about building and maintaining substantive knowledge of current web technologies, with significant interpretive effort in synthesizing and judging which trends, practices, and information sources are relevant and applicable.

title: Web Developers
task: Analyze user needs to determine technical requirements.
rationale: Analyzing user needs to determine technical requirements is dominated by Interpretation (translating ambiguous user desires into concrete specs) and Knowledge (understanding what is technically feasible), with minor contributions from other channels.

title: Web Developers
task: Monitor security system performance logs to identify problems and notify security specialists when problems occur.
rationale: This task centers on interpreting security log data to distinguish meaningful problems from noise, controlling a monitoring workflow/process, and applying domain knowledge of security systems — with modest technical skill in navigating log tools and minimal credential or relational components.

title: Web Developers
task: Respond to user email inquiries, or set up automated systems to send responses.
rationale: This task splits between Technical (coding automated email response systems) and Process (managing the workflow of inquiry handling), with moderate Interpretation (translating user needs into appropriate response logic) and minor Relational (ongoing accountability for communication quality).

title: Web Developers
task: Renew domain name registrations.
rationale: Domain renewal is primarily a routine administrative workflow task (navigating registrar portals, managing credentials, tracking expiry dates), with meaningful access rent from controlling login credentials and registrar relationships, minor technical execution, and some relational accountability for ensuring continuity of client web presence.

title: Web Developers
task: Confer with management or development teams to prioritize needs, resolve conflicts, develop content criteria, or choose solutions.
rationale: This conferral task centers on synthesizing competing needs and resolving ambiguity (Interpretation), managing the decision-making workflow (Process), and maintaining stakeholder accountability (Relational), with modest domain knowledge to inform trade-offs.

title: Web Developers
task: Communicate with network personnel or Web site hosting agencies to address hardware or software issues affecting Web sites.
rationale: This communication/coordination task earns rent primarily through Interpretation (diagnosing and articulating technical issues across teams), Knowledge (understanding hardware/software interactions), and Access (knowing who to contact and navigating vendor/hosting relationships), with moderate Process control over the resolution workflow.

title: Web Developers
task: Collaborate with management or users to develop e-commerce strategies and to integrate these strategies with Web sites.
rationale: This task centers on synthesizing business goals, user needs, and technical constraints into coherent e-commerce strategies (Interpretation), supported by domain knowledge of e-commerce and web technologies, with moderate process coordination and some relational accountability to management/users.

title: Web Developers
task: Document test plans, testing procedures, or test results.
rationale: Documenting test plans and results is primarily a process-control and technical writing task—structuring workflows, organizing test artifacts, and producing clear written records—with meaningful interpretation in synthesizing test outcomes into coherent documentation.

title: Web Developers
task: Establish appropriate server directory trees.
rationale: Establishing server directory trees is primarily a process-control and workflow-organization task requiring knowledge of web server conventions (document roots, permissions, virtual hosts) and technical execution of the setup, with moderate interpretation to map project requirements to an appropriate structure.

title: Web Developers
task: Recommend and implement performance improvements.
rationale: Recommending performance improvements requires interpreting profiling data and synthesizing diagnosis into actionable solutions (I), implementing them demands hands-on coding and deployment skill (T), and knowing which optimization techniques apply requires substantive domain knowledge (K), with minor process overhead for managing the improvement cycle.

title: Web Developers
task: Document technical factors such as server load, bandwidth, database performance, and browser and device types.
rationale: Documenting technical factors like server load and database performance draws primarily on substantive domain knowledge of these systems, interpretation to synthesize and structure findings meaningfully, and process control to establish and maintain documentation workflows, with modest technical execution in gathering and recording metrics.

title: Web Developers
task: Develop or implement procedures for ongoing Web site revision.
rationale: Developing and implementing revision procedures is primarily about controlling and structuring a repeatable workflow (Process), with meaningful judgment in designing those procedures (Interpretation) and some technical skill in implementing them, while knowledge and relational factors play minor supporting roles.

title: Web Developers
task: Create Web models or prototypes that include physical, interface, logical, or data models.
rationale: Creating web models and prototypes requires heavy interpretation (translating requirements into physical, interface, logical, and data models) and technical execution (building the actual prototype artifacts), with supporting domain knowledge of modeling conventions and minor process overhead.

title: Web Developers
task: Provide clear, detailed descriptions of Web site specifications, such as product features, activities, software, communication protocols, programming languages, and operating systems software and hardware.
rationale: This task centers on synthesizing technical knowledge into clear specifications (Interpretation), drawing heavily on domain knowledge of web technologies, protocols, and languages (Knowledge), with minor process control over documentation workflows and light relational accountability to stakeholders.

title: Web Developers
task: Evaluate or recommend server hardware or software.
rationale: Evaluating and recommending server hardware/software is primarily driven by substantive knowledge of server technologies and the interpretive judgment needed to synthesize options against specific requirements, with minor technical weight for hands-on assessment.

title: Web Developers
task: Install and configure hypertext transfer protocol (HTTP) servers and associated operating systems.
rationale: Installing and configuring HTTP servers (e.g., Apache, Nginx) and operating systems is primarily a hands-on technical execution task requiring substantive knowledge of server software, networking, and OS configuration, with some process control over the deployment workflow.

title: Web Developers
task: Research, document, rate, or select alternatives for Web architecture or technologies.
rationale: Researching and selecting web architecture alternatives is dominated by synthesis and judgment (Interpretation) across competing options and trade-offs, backed by substantive domain knowledge of technologies, with minor process overhead for documenting and rating alternatives.

title: Web Developers
task: Develop system interaction or sequence diagrams.
rationale: Developing system interaction/sequence diagrams is primarily an interpretive act—translating system requirements and architecture into structured visual specifications—combined with technical execution in diagramming tools or notation, plus domain knowledge of system design patterns.

title: Web Developers
task: Design and implement Web site security measures, such as firewalls and message encryption.
rationale: Security design requires deep domain knowledge of threats, protocols, and cryptographic methods (K), judgment in selecting and configuring appropriate measures for the specific site context (I), and hands-on technical implementation of firewalls, encryption, and configurations (T), with minor process overhead for deployment pipelines.

title: Web Developers
task: Incorporate technical considerations into Web site design plans, such as budgets, equipment, performance requirements, and legal issues including accessibility and privacy.
rationale: This task centers on synthesizing diverse technical constraints (budgets, performance, accessibility, privacy law) into coherent design plans, making Interpretation dominant, supported by substantive domain Knowledge of web standards and legal requirements, with minor process oversight and little hands-on coding.
